answer: kubectl assumes it is running inside a Kubernetes cluster if there is a Kubernetes service account token file mounted at /var/run/secrets/kubernetes.io/serviceaccount/token, and the KUBERNETES_SERVICE_HOST environment variable is set, and the KUBERNETES_SERVICE_PORT environment variable is set, and you don't explicitly specify a namespace on the kubectl command line. In this case, kubectl looks up the namespace of that ServiceAccount (the same as the namespace of the Pod) and acts against that namespace. If the POD_NAMESPACE environment variable is set, cli operations on namespaced resources will default to the variable value. Explicit use of --namespace <value> overrides this behavior.

kubectl allows resource types to be specified using singular, plural, or abbreviated forms, and these are case-insensitive. For example, "pod", "pods", and "po" all refer to the same resource type. When the resource name is omitted in a command, kubectl displays details for all resources of the specified type.
The default output format is human readable plain-text. The '-o wide' output format is also plain-text but includes additional information; for pods, the node name is included.

The '--server-print=false' flag would be necessary when you want to disable receiving specific column information from the server about objects, so that the client prints the output instead. The expected output when this flag is used is that the client prints the information, which may be less consistent with other clients, and for example, when printing information about the status of a pod, the output is similar to:

NAME       AGE
pod-name   1m
